slides.title: Introduction to Lines in Geometry (Advanced)
slides.text:
In geometry, a line is a straight path that extends infinitely in both directions without ending.
We represent lines with arrows on both ends.
Today, we will study how lines relate to each other when they are on the same flat surface.
For instance, consider the example of Line vs Line Segment:
A line segment has two fixed endpoints (like a ruler's edge), while a line extends forever in both directions.
Another case is the study of Naming Lines:
We name a line using two points on it, like line AB, or with a single lowercase letter, like line l.
How does the concept of Introduction to Lines in Geometry (Advanced) help us think critically?

examples.title: Line vs Line Segment
examples.content: A line segment has two fixed endpoints (like a ruler's edge), while a line extends forever in both directions.

examples.title: Naming Lines
examples.content: We name a line using two points on it, like line AB, or with a single lowercase letter, like line l.

slides.title: What are Parallel Lines? (Advanced)
slides.text:
Parallel lines are lines on the same flat surface that are always the same distance apart (equidistant) and never meet or cross each other, no matter how far they extend.
This slide introduces key insights about What are Parallel Lines? (Advanced).
For instance, consider the example of Railway Tracks:
The two rails of a straight train track are parallel. They must never cross, or the train would derail.
Another case is the study of Lined Paper:
The horizontal blue lines on your writing notebook are parallel lines.
How does the concept of What are Parallel Lines? (Advanced) help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Railway Tracks
examples.content: The two rails of a straight train track are parallel. They must never cross, or the train would derail.

examples.title: Lined Paper
examples.content: The horizontal blue lines on your writing notebook are parallel lines.

slides.title: Identifying Parallel Lines (Advanced)
slides.text:
We use the symbol || to show that two lines are parallel.
For example, if line AB is parallel to line CD, we write AB || CD.
They will never intersect.
For instance, consider the example of Opposite Edges:
The top and bottom edges of a rectangular blackboard or tablet screen are parallel.
Another case is the study of Window Grids:
Vertical bars in a window grid are parallel to each other to form a protective cage.
How does the concept of Identifying Parallel Lines (Advanced) help us think critically?

examples.title: Opposite Edges
examples.content: The top and bottom edges of a rectangular blackboard or tablet screen are parallel.

examples.title: Window Grids
examples.content: Vertical bars in a window grid are parallel to each other to form a protective cage.

slides.title: What are Intersecting Lines? (Advanced)
slides.text:
Intersecting lines are lines that cross each other at exactly one point.
The point where they cross is called the point of intersection.
When they cross, they form angles.
For instance, consider the example of Crossroads:
A street intersection where two roads cross each other represents intersecting lines.
Another case is the study of Opened Scissors:
The two blades of an opened pair of scissors form intersecting lines crossing at the central pivot screw.
How does the concept of What are Intersecting Lines? (Advanced) help us think critically?

examples.title: Crossroads
examples.content: A street intersection where two roads cross each other represents intersecting lines.

examples.title: Opened Scissors
examples.content: The two blades of an opened pair of scissors form intersecting lines crossing at the central pivot screw.

slides.title: What are Perpendicular Lines? (Advanced)
slides.text:
Perpendicular lines are a special type of intersecting lines that cross each other to form exactly four right angles (90 degrees).
The symbol for perpendicular is ⊥.
For instance, consider the example of Perfect Cross:
A plus sign (+) or a letter 'T' forms perpendicular lines. The angles formed are exactly 90°.
Another case is the study of Wall and Floor:
A vertical wall meets the flat floor perpendicularly to ensure the building is stable.
How does the concept of What are Perpendicular Lines? (Advanced) help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Perfect Cross
examples.content: A plus sign (+) or a letter 'T' forms perpendicular lines. The angles formed are exactly 90°.

examples.title: Wall and Floor
examples.content: A vertical wall meets the flat floor perpendicularly to ensure the building is stable.

slides.title: Identifying Perpendicular Lines (Advanced)
slides.text:
If line AB is perpendicular to line CD, we write AB ⊥ CD.
You can verify this by placing a protractor or the corner of a sheet of paper at the intersection to see if it fits perfectly.
For instance, consider the example of Corner Check:
The corner of your textbook is formed by two perpendicular lines meeting at a 90° angle.
Another case is the study of Crossroad Corners:
A grid-style street layout has roads intersecting perpendicularly, creating 90-degree corners.
How does the concept of Identifying Perpendicular Lines (Advanced) help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Corner Check
examples.content: The corner of your textbook is formed by two perpendicular lines meeting at a 90° angle.

examples.title: Crossroad Corners
examples.content: A grid-style street layout has roads intersecting perpendicularly, creating 90-degree corners.

slides.title: Lines in the Real World (Advanced)
slides.text:
Architects, engineers, and designers use parallel, perpendicular, and intersecting lines to create houses, bridges, and beautiful artworks.
Look around and find these lines!.
For instance, consider the example of Eiffel Tower:
The Eiffel Tower contains intersecting support beams and parallel vertical columns.
Another case is the study of Bridges:
Suspension bridges use parallel vertical cables and perpendicular steel trusses.
How does the concept of Lines in the Real World (Advanced) help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Eiffel Tower
examples.content: The Eiffel Tower contains intersecting support beams and parallel vertical columns.

examples.title: Bridges
examples.content: Suspension bridges use parallel vertical cables and perpendicular steel trusses.

slides.title: Drawing Geometric Lines (Advanced)
slides.text:
To draw parallel or perpendicular lines, we use a ruler (straightedge) and a set square (triangle ruler).
A set square helps us align a perfect 90-degree angle.
For instance, consider the example of Drawing Parallel Lines:
Slide a ruler along a fixed edge to draw two lines that maintain the same distance.
Another case is the study of Drawing Perpendiculars:
Place the 90° corner of a set square against a line, then draw along the vertical edge.
How does the concept of Drawing Geometric Lines (Advanced) help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Drawing Parallel Lines
examples.content: Slide a ruler along a fixed edge to draw two lines that maintain the same distance.

examples.title: Drawing Perpendiculars
examples.content: Place the 90° corner of a set square against a line, then draw along the vertical edge.

slides.title: Summary: Geometric Lines (Advanced)
slides.text:
Superb! You have mastered lines in geometry: Parallel lines (||, never meet), Intersecting lines (cross at a point), and Perpendicular lines (⊥, cross at exactly 90 degrees).
These concepts are the foundation of shape study and drafting.
For instance, consider the example of Key Symbols:
|| means Parallel. ⊥ means Perpendicular. Vertex: Intersection point.
Another case is the study of Daily Practice:
Scan your room today: count 5 parallel lines and 5 perpendicular lines in your furniture!
How does the concept of Summary: Geometric Lines (Advanced) help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Key Symbols
examples.content: || means Parallel. ⊥ means Perpendicular. Vertex: Intersection point.

examples.title: Daily Practice
examples.content: Scan your room today: count 5 parallel lines and 5 perpendicular lines in your furniture!

standard.q: What are parallel lines?
options: Lines that cross at exactly 90 degrees.
Lines that will never meet or cross.

standard.q: Which symbol represents that two lines are parallel?
What are perpendicular lines?
options: Lines that cross to form exactly 90-degree angles.
Lines that go in opposite directions.

standard.q: Which symbol represents that two lines are perpendicular?
What is the point where two intersecting lines cross called?
Which of the following is a real-world example of parallel lines?
Which of the following represents perpendicular lines?
options: The rails of a train track
The opposite sides of a book
The hands of a clock showing 12:20

standard.q: If two lines cross each other at a 45-degree angle, they are:
How many degrees are in the angles formed by perpendicular lines?
If line L1 || L2, and L1 is extended by 10 meters, will they cross?
options: Only if L2 is also extended.

standard.q: Which capital letter has parallel lines?
Which capital letter has perpendicular lines but no parallel lines?
Which capital letter contains intersecting lines that are NOT perpendicular?
Which tool is best for checking if two drawn lines are perpendicular?
How many intersection points do two parallel lines have?
How many intersection points do two intersecting lines have?
If line AB ⊥ CD, how many right angles are formed around the intersection?
Which of the following shapes has opposite sides that are parallel?
Which of the following shapes has adjacent sides that are perpendicular?
What type of lines are formed by the letter 'V'?
Which of the following is a property of a line?
options: It extends infinitely in both directions.

standard.q: Which of the following is a property of a line segment?
options: It has exactly two endpoints and a fixed length.
It has arrows on both ends.

standard.q: Which of the following statements is true?
options: Statement A: All perpendicular lines are intersecting, but not all intersecting lines are perpendicular.
Statement B: All intersecting lines are perpendicular, but not all perpendicular lines are intersecting.

standard.q: What geometric term describes lines that are in the same plane and never cross? (use lowercase)
Which letter has both parallel and perpendicular lines?

challenge.q: If line A || line B, and line B ⊥ line C, what is the relationship between line A and line C?
How many pairs of parallel lines are there in a regular hexagon?
In a right-angled triangle, how many pairs of perpendicular sides are there?
Which of the following statements is true?
options: Statement A: If two lines are parallel to the same line, they are parallel to each other.
Statement B: If two lines are perpendicular to the same line, they are perpendicular to each other.

challenge.q: How many points of intersection are formed by three mutually intersecting lines that do not pass through the same point?

performance.title: Drawing and Classifying Lines
performance.desc: Use a ruler to draw a line segment and label it AB. Then, draw a line CD parallel to AB (AB || CD). Next, draw a line EF perpendicular to AB (EF ⊥ AB) crossing both lines. Label all points of intersection. Color parallel lines blue and perpendicular lines red. Show it to your parent.
labels: Correct drawing of parallel lines labeled AB and CD
Correct drawing of perpendicular line EF forming 90° angles
Neatness, color coding, and correct labeling of points

pages:
Exercise 1: Drawing Parallel and Perpendicular Lines

Use your stylus to draw a parallel line and a perpendicular line for each line segment below.

Draw a line Parallel to AB:
[Parallel line drawn parallel to AB] A B
Draw a line Perpendicular to CD:
[Perpendicular line drawn at 90° to CD] C D

Exercise 2: Classifying Lines in Capital Letters

Look at the capital letters below. State what types of lines they contain (Parallel, Perpendicular, or Intersecting but not Perpendicular) using your stylus.

1. The letter E contains:
Parallel and Perpendicular lines
2. The letter X contains:
Intersecting but not Perpendicular lines
3. The letter M contains:
Parallel and Intersecting but not Perpendicular lines

Exercise 3: Line Riddles

Write the correct answer to the geometric riddles below using your stylus.

1. We are a pair of lines. We are in the same plane, we go in the same direction, and we never cross. What are we?
Parallel lines
2. We cross each other and form four right angles. What special lines are we?
Perpendicular lines
